children.name: Gymnorhina
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the Australian Magpie, one of Australia's most iconic and familiar birds. Despite its name, it is not closely related to the Northern Hemisphere magpies but belongs to the butcherbird family. It is a large, boldly patterned black-and-white bird with a famously complex and melodious carolling song, and is renowned for its territorial swooping during breeding season.

children.name: Gymnorhina tibicen
children.description: A large, black-and-white bird with a powerful silver bill and an extraordinarily rich, flute-like carolling song that is considered the most musical of Australian birds, found across Australia and southern New Guinea. Each individual has a unique song dialect learnt from its neighbours. It is highly territorial and well known for its springtime swooping of pedestrians and cyclists near nests.

children.name: Cracticus
children.description: The butcherbirds — a genus of medium-sized, stocky passerines with strongly hooked bills bearing a distinct tooth-like notch, an adaptation for their predatory feeding habits. They earned their common name from their habit of impaling prey on thorns or wedging it in tree forks as a 'larder'. They are accomplished songsters with rich, flute-like calls.

children.name: Cracticus nigrogularis
children.description: A striking black-and-white butcherbird with a black throat and a black hood, its rich, musical, flute-like song is one of the finest bird songs in Australia, heard across much of the continent. It hunts from an exposed perch, dropping onto prey such as insects, lizards, and small mammals. It is named for its habit of securing uneaten prey on thorns for later consumption.

children.name: Cracticus torquatus
children.description: A grey-backed butcherbird with a white collar and black head, found across Australia wherever there are trees. Despite its small size for a butcherbird, it is a ferocious predator that readily takes large insects, small vertebrates, and even other birds. Its clear, rolling, whistled song is a familiar sound in Australian woodlands and gardens.

children.name: Cracticus quoyi
children.description: A large, entirely black butcherbird with a heavy blue-grey hooked bill, found in lowland forests and mangroves of northern Australia, New Guinea, and nearby islands. It is a powerful predator that takes large insects, small mammals, birds, and reptiles. Its loud, flute-like song is a characteristic sound of New Guinea lowland forests.

children.name: Cracticus argenteus
children.description: A handsome butcherbird with silver-grey upperparts, a white collar, and black head, found in dry woodlands of northern and western Australia. It is similar to the Grey Butcherbird but paler, with a more restricted distribution in the tropical north. Its rich, whistled song carries across the open woodlands.

children.name: Cracticus cassicus
children.description: A striking black-and-white butcherbird with a black hood, white nape, and white underparts, found in lowland forests of New Guinea and the Moluccas. It has a powerful hooked bill and feeds on large insects and small vertebrates. Its song is a rich, melodious whistle.

children.name: Cracticus louisiadensis
children.description: A little-known butcherbird endemic to Tagula Island in the Louisiade Archipelago of Papua New Guinea. It has black upperparts and white underparts with a black breast band. Its restricted island distribution makes it one of the least studied members of the genus.

children.name: Cracticus mentalis
children.description: A medium-sized butcherbird with black upperparts, a white collar, and white underparts, found in savanna woodlands of southern New Guinea and northern Cape York Peninsula in Australia. It is a vocal species with a rich, whistled song, often heard delivering melodic phrases from exposed perches.

children.name: Artamus
children.description: The woodswallows — a genus of highly social, aerial insectivores with a superficial resemblance to swallows, for which they are named. They have broad, triangular wings, short tails, and stout, finch-like bills adapted for catching insects on the wing. They are among the most gregarious of all Australasian passerines, often packing tightly together on exposed branches in large, noisy flocks.

children.name: Artamus leucorynchus
children.description: A medium-sized woodswallow with dark grey upperparts, a pure white breast and belly, and a pale blue bill, found from Southeast Asia through Indonesia to Australia. It is a highly social aerial feeder, catching insects with graceful, swooping flight often high above the canopy. It roosts communally in tight clusters on exposed branches.

children.name: Artamus cyanopterus
children.description: A dark, smoky-grey woodswallow with a distinctive white wing-bar that is conspicuous in flight, common across southern and eastern Australia. It feeds almost entirely on the wing, catching insects in sweeping, graceful flight over forests and heathlands. Like all woodswallows, it is highly gregarious, sitting in huddled rows on dead branches and engaging in noisy pre-roosting displays.

children.name: Artamus maximus
children.description: The largest woodswallow, with dark grey upperparts, a pale grey belly, and a thick silver bill, found in montane forests of New Guinea. It is a powerful flier that catches insects on the wing, often seen soaring high above the canopy. It roosts communally in small groups on exposed dead branches.

children.name: Artamus monachus
children.description: A striking woodswallow with dark grey upperparts offset by a prominent white patch on the lower back, and white underparts, endemic to Sulawesi and neighbouring islands in Indonesia. It is a social aerial insectivore, often seen in small flocks hawking for insects over forest and open country.

children.name: Artamus insignis
children.description: A medium-sized woodswallow with dark grey upperparts and a white belly, endemic to the Bismarck Archipelago of Papua New Guinea. It is a little-known species of lowland forests and edges, where it hawks for insects in small, social flocks.

children.name: Artamus personatus
children.description: A small, nomadic woodswallow with dark grey upperparts, a distinctive black face mask in the male, and pale grey underparts, found across much of mainland Australia. It is highly nomadic, moving in large, erratic flocks in response to insect abundance. It often roosts in dense, tight clusters on exposed branches.

children.name: Artamus superciliosus
children.description: A striking woodswallow with dark grey upperparts, a bold white eyebrow, a chestnut breast, and white underparts, found across eastern and southern Australia. It is highly nomadic, moving in large flocks in search of insect swarms. Its name comes from the prominent white supercilium that contrasts with its dark head.

children.name: Artamus cinereus
children.description: A medium-sized woodswallow with dark grey upperparts, a black face mask, a pale grey throat, and white underparts, found across northern and central Australia and in southern New Guinea. It is a common and conspicuous species of open woodlands, often seen perched on roadside wires in small, tight groups.

children.name: Artamus minor
children.description: The smallest woodswallow, with dark grey-brown upperparts, a pale throat, and a white belly, found across much of mainland Australia. It is a highly social, nomadic species that travels in noisy flocks, hawking for insects over open country. It roosts communally in dense clusters on branches, often huddled together even in the heat of the day.

children.name: Artamus flaviventer
children.description: A woodswallow with yellow vent from the Moluccas and New Guinea.

children.name: Artamus melanops
children.description: A woodswallow with black face from the Lesser Sunda Islands.

children.name: Artamus monachus
children.description: A woodswallow with ivory back from Sulawesi.

children.name: Artamus maximus
children.description: The largest woodswallow from the montane forests of New Guinea.

children.name: Artamus melanops
children.description: A woodswallow from the Lesser Sunda Islands.

children.name: Artamus flaviventer
children.description: A woodswallow from the Moluccas.

children.name: Artamus maximus
children.description: The largest woodswallow from New Guinea.
